coredata.dc:description: This present work examines the corrosion inhibitory potential of new ultravanadyl glasses, made using the traditional melt-quenching method, with nominal compositions (0.25-x) Bi2O3 - x Nb2O5- 0.75V2O5 for 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.15. The X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) was employed to identify the sample's phase. Structural investigation was achieved using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. The physical parameters namely density (ρ), volume (Vm), oxygen packing density (OPD) and ionic concentration (N) of the glassy matrix were studied in relation to the substitution of Bi2O3 by Nb2O5. The inorganic compounds were evaluated in this work for their ability to inhibit corrosion of mild steel in 1.0 M HCl. These agents interact with the metal surface, forming a protective layer that reduces corrosion rates. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) analyses were used to monitor their performance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This present work examines the corrosion inhibitory potential of new ultravanadyl glasses, made using the traditional melt-quenching method, with nominal compositions (0.25-x) Bi2O3 - x Nb2O5- 0.75V2O5 for 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.15. The X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) was employed to identify the sample's phase. Structural investigation was achieved using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. The physical parameters namely density (ρ), volume (Vm), oxygen packing density (OPD) and ionic concentration (N) of the glassy matrix were studied in relation to the substitution of Bi2O3 by Nb2O5. The inorganic compounds were evaluated in this work for their ability to inhibit corrosion of mild steel in 1.0 M HCl. These agents interact with the metal surface, forming a protective layer that reduces corrosion rates. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) analyses were used to monitor their performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Vanadium-based glasses have special physical properties, setting them apart from other glasses, such as a good UV transmission, low melting and softening temper temperatures [1,2]. The current glass technology containing Bi2O3 has allowed us to achieve outstanding performance in the optoelectronic field, for instance the industrial and medical lasers, anti-reflective coatings for solar collectors etc [3,4]. Corrosion is a natural mechanism in which any unstable metal interacts with its environment to create a more stable substance on the surface. This stable by-product is the result of a chemical process that causes a reduction in the metal used to judge corrosion. Due to thermal and hydric fluctuations, metals are mainly affected by environmental deficits. Another peculiarity of corrosion is that it does not manifest itself homogeneously in all circumstances [5]. Thanks to its mechanical strength and productiveness, Mild steel is an iron alloy that finds extensive use in a variety of sectors. Nonetheless, a notable obstacle that causes hassle for manufacturers is its soared vulnerability to corrosion [6,7]. Hydrochloric acid is widely used in various industrial operations. Varies inquiries were made about the numerous corrosive attributes and outcomes of this solution [8–11]. The corrosion of steel under acidic conditions has long attracted considerable attention from researchers due to its significance in both industrial and academic contexts. Recent advances suggest that transition-metal oxides, particularly V2O5, can strongly influence surface reactivity and passive film formation through changes in network connectivity and vanadium oxidation state [1,3]. However, a clear structure–property relationship linking glass composition and electrochemical corrosion performance remains insufficiently established.

This study aims to synthesize novel glasses based on vanadium oxide V2O5 and to characterize them using various analytical techniques such as XRD, density, molar volume, Raman an IR spectroscopy of glasses of the system: (0.25-x) Bi2O3 - x Nb2O5- 0.75 V2O5 for 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.15. The synthesized compounds made in this work were then examined for their corrosion-inhibiting power. The analysis of Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and Stationary Polarization (PDP) were employed to examine the behavior of the synthesized vanadate-based glasses. The correlation between the composition and structure of the prepared glasses and their corrosion performance has been established and elaborated in this work.

The glass samples of the Bi2O3–Nb2O5 – V2O5 system were synthesized from stoichiometric proportions of bismuth oxide (Bi2O3, >99.5 % purity, Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd.), niobium pentoxide (Nb2O5, >99.5 % purity, Fluka), and vanadium oxide (V2O5, >99.5 % purity, Sigma-Aldrich). The starting materials were weighed and carefully ground in an agate mortar in order to make the reaction mixture uniform. The latter is then ground after spending a whole night, placed in alumina crucibles, in the oven at 350 °C. After 30 min of gradual heating at 1000 °C, the molten substance was quenched in open air. Table 1 represents the compositions of the glasses and the name given to them to simplify their notion in the article.
The compositions are achieved as follow: in the glasses system: (0.25-x) Bi2O3 - x Nb2O5- 0.75V2O5 for 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.15.
The compositions of the prepared compounds were formulated as follows, and they were designated as P1, P2, P3, and P4 for simplicity of reference:

For x = 0, the composition of compound P1 is 0.25 Bi2O3 – 0.75 V2O5. To express the composition as a single formula, we multiply the coefficient in front of each oxide by the subscript of each element. For exemple.
-
For Bi: 0.25 × 2 = 0.5
-
For V: 0.75 × 2 = 1.5
-
For O: (0.25 × 3) + (0.75 × 5) = 0.75 + 3.75 = 4.5
Therefore, the composition of P1 is Bi0·5V1·5O4.5.
So, the composition of P2 is Bi0·4Nb0·1V1·5O4.6.

For x = 0.10, the composition of compound P3 is 0.15 Bi2O3 – 0.10 Nb2O5 -0.75 V2O5. The process was carried out the same.
-
For Bi: 0.15 × 2 = 0.3
-
For V: 0.75 × 2 = 1.5
-
For Nb: 0.10 × 2 = 0.2
-
For O: (0.15 × 3) + (0.10 × 5) + (0.75 × 5) = 0.45 + 0.5+ 3.75 = 4.7
Hence the composition of P3 is Bi0·3Nb0·2V1·5O4.7.

Finally for x = 0.15, the composition of compound P4 is 0.10 Bi2O3 – 0.15 Nb2O5 -0.75 V2O5. We followed the same steps.
-
For Bi: 0.10 × 2 = 0.2
-
For V: 0.75 × 2 = 1.5
-
For Nb: 0.15 × 2 = 0.3
-
For O: (0.10 × 3) + (0.15 × 5) + (0.75 × 5) = 0.30 + 0.75 + 3.75 = 4.8
Therefore, the composition of P4 is Bi0·2Nb0·3V1·5O4.8.

The Archimedes thrust principle has served as the foundation for the density measurements (ρ) of the examined materials. They were conducted with a diethylorthophthalate densimeter at 25 °C. Each density determination was repeated three times, and the average value was calculated accordingly. The observed density values had an accuracy of about 0.03 g/cm3. The density ρ was used to estimate the molar volume Vm.

The glass system is characterized by various parameters that enable us to evaluate its overall behavior and gain deeper insight into its structural arrangement and the positioning of its constituent ions. The ionic concentrations of the prepared compounds (N) [12], the inter-ionic distance (Ri) [13], and the OPD [14] are computed using the following relationships (1), (2), (3):

n (O) is the number of oxygen atoms in the composition, M is the molar mass, y is the molar fraction in the composition of the glass and N is the ionic concentrations.

The relationship between the ionic concentration (N), the inter-ionic distance (Ri) in glass materials is interconnected through the structural and optical characteristics of the glass network. As the ionic concentration (N) increases, the number of ions per unit volume becomes higher, which consequently shortens the mean interionic distance between neighboring ions (Ri).

The parameter Vd reflects the stiffness of the oxide network [15] and is evaluated according to the equation below (4):

In this formula, Vi corresponds to the packing factor and Xi ​to the mole fraction of each component. The Vi of Bi2O3, Nb2O5 and V2O5 is available in the literature [16]. Table 2 illustrates the mole fraction Xi and the filling factor Vi for each component.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were performed using a Siemens D5000 diffractometer (Siemens, Germany) equipped with Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5406 Å), operated at 40 kV and 30 mA.

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using an ALPHA spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) operating in attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The powdered glass samples were placed directly on the ATR crystal to ensure proper optical contact. Spectra were recorded between 400 and 4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

Raman spectra were acquired at room temperature using a XRD Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with an argon ion laser (λ = 785 nm) serving as the excitation source. Spectra were obtained in the 1400–100 cm−1 range with a spectral resolution of 1–2 cm−1.

Electrochemical analyses were carried out using a standard three-electrode system composed of a platinum counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) serving as the reference, and a mild steel sample as the working electrode. A PGZ 100 Potentiostat is utilized for stationary measurements in potentiodynamic mode. All electrodes were immersed in 1 M HCl contained in a glass electrolyte cell. The three-electrode assembly was connected to a potentiostat controlled by a computer for data acquisition. The chemical composition of the mild steel used as the working electrode is shown in Table 3 .
A 1 M hydrochloric acid solution, produced through diluting concentrated acid with distilled water, serves as the corrosive medium. Every experiment was conducted at 298 K.

The electrochemical methods and scientific software employed in this study are consistent with the methodology used in our previous research, ensuring reliability and continuity across both investigations. Potentiodynamic polarization measurements were carried out using a PGZ100 potentiostat/galvanostat, operated with VoltaMaster 4 software. This technique is commonly applied in corrosion studies to analyze anodic and cathodic reactions at the metal-electrolyte interface. The three-electrode system enables electrochemical measurements. The linear Tafel slopes are estimated to determine the corrosion current density (icorr). The inhibition efficiency was evaluated using the subsequent equation (Eq. (5)) [5]:
(5)
E % =
(

where i0corr is the corrosion current density without the inhibitor and icorr is with inhibitor.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted at open circuit potential over a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz, using a sinusoidal AC signal with a 10-mV amplitude. This technique is commonly employed to evaluate charge transfer resistance and the formation of protective films on metal surfaces. The resulting EIS data were represented using an equivalent circuit (Fig. 1 ) [17,18]. A solution resistor (Rs), a charge transfer resistor (Rp), and a constant-phase element (CPE) constitute the corresponding circuit. The inhibition efficiency (E%) based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements was calculated using equation (6) below [15]:
(6)

where Rct and Roct are the charge-transfer resistances of the blank (uninhibited) and inhibited systems, respectively.

The effective double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was determined using the equation presented below:

XRD analysis is the approach used to identify the various phases in a system. Fig. 2 shows the absence of diffraction lines in the spectrum of this glass. Additionally, the figure displays a broad scattering feature at low angles (2θ ≈ 28°) with no sharp diffraction peaks. In fact, large visible bumps are created by grape distributions linked to disorder in these materials. This demonstrates the amorphous nature of the prepared glass.

The oxygen packing density (OPD) demonstrates how compact the glass network is and how the oxygen atoms are arranged in treated glasses. This parameter reflects the compactness of the glass network and influences its structural properties, including rigidity, connectivity, and overall network arrangement [19]. Fig. 4 shows that OPD values decrease as niobium ion concentration increases. These data indicate the glasses exhibit reduced compactness and density as the Nb2O5 content increases. In addition, this variation implies that network polymerization occurs due to the presence of non-ponton oxygen and the lack of oxygen bridge creation with the introduction of Nb2O5.

The infrared spectra of (0.25-x) Bi2O3 - x Nb2O5- 0.75V2O5 glasses are shown in Fig. 5 . The FTIR spectra of the glasses prepared exhibit broad and weak absorption features rather than well-defined peaks, particularly for compositions with high V2O5 content. This behavior is typical of vanadate-rich glasses and is attributed to the dominance of disordered VOx structural units and strong electronic absorption of vanadium ions. Therefore, the assignments discussed below should be regarded as tentative and indicative of general structural motifs rather than precise vibrational modes [1,3,4]. FTIR analysis is used for identifying which functional groups occur in the synthesized materials. The spectra of (0.25-x) Bi2O3 - x Nb2O5- 0.75V2O5 with 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.15 in the 400-1600 cm−1 frequency region are shown in Fig. 5. They consist of characteristic frequency bands. The band around 450 - 500 cm−1 is attributed to Bi–O–Bi units [20]. The bending oscillation of the Bi–O that links the oxygen in the pyramidal [BiO3] and octahedral [BiO6] octahedral units causes the band visible right around 650–700 cm−1 [21]. The bands recorded around 800- 850 cm−1 could be attributed to the vibration of the NbO band [22]. An elongation vibration of the VO band type non-bridge of the VO5 trigonal bipyramids is associated to the band observed around 950 - 1000 cm−1 [23]. The frail band around 1100 cm−1 can be attributed to V–O bonds [24]. The latter two bands have a constant intensity, as vanadium remains constant along the line. The IR band's width and intensity grow in tandem with the fraction of Nb in the glass lattice because the Nb5+ ions act more as structural modifiers. This results in a notable increase of NBO atoms or depolymerization, both of which have an impact on the glass system's structural stability.

Raman spectroscopy is a valuable technique used elucidate the molecular structure of substances, such as the arrangement of atoms or the shifts in chemical bonding. Additionally, it may determine the glass sample's maximum phonon energy and important chemical linkages [25]. Using this approach, the structural modifications of the glass network induced by varying Nb2O5 content can be evaluated.

Fig. 6 shows the Raman spectra of samples prepared. The intense extended bands observed between 135 and 140 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrations of the metal cation Bi3+ within the octahedral [BiO6] units [26]. The bands detected at 190 cm−1 are attributable to the vibration of Nb–O–Nb bonds [27]. Bi–O oscillation of tetrahedral [BiO6] units [26] is associated with the peak noted at 275 cm−1. The extension oscillation of the Bi–O–Bi ties inside the [BiO6] and [BiO3] units is represented by the bands between 400 and 410 cm−1 [21,28]. The vibrational modes caused by the interactions of the bismuth and oxygen atoms within these vitreous structure units are indicated by these spectral bands. The stretching vibrations of the Bi–O bonds inside the [BiO6] units may be attributed to the spectral bands observed in the 510–525 cm−1 range [29]. Furthermore, we observe that a band originating from a combination of the interactions of the V–O–V groups vibrations emerges at about 700 cm−1 [30]. It can also function as a network trainer at high V2O5 concentrations. The NbO band's vibration could be responsible for the bands spotted at about 815 cm−1 [22]. An elongation vibration of the non-bridging VO bond of VO5 trigonal bipyramids may be associated with the band at 987 cm−1 [31]. The impact of cation substitution is shown by variations in the frequency and intensity of the related lines. We notice the clear shift of octahedral units Bi–O–Bi and Bi–O to lower frequencies. The intensity of their bands decreases significantly, some even disappearing such as the bands observed at 515 cm−1 and 400 cm−1 as noticed in Fig. 6. This structural change is caused by the addition of the bivalent cation Nb5+ to the glass structure. When Nb2O5 is introduced, the peaks have moved from their typical positions. This change could be regarded as a significant alteration in the vitreous structure of the compounds. The band at the 1000 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of terminal VO bonds in VO5 units shows a marked decrease in intensity upon Nb2O5 addition, despite constant V2O5 content. This behavior is associated with a reduction in the polarizability change of the VO bond and a distortion of the VO5 local symmetry induced by the formation of V–O–Nb linkages. The incorporation of NbO6 leads to weakened and less Raman-active VO vibrations [32].

Fig. 7 shows the time-dependent behavior of the open circuit potential for mild steel immersed in a 1.0 M HCl solution for 30 min, both in the absence and presence of inhibitors P1, P2, P3, and P4 at different concentrations. The observed OCP variations reflect the combined effects of anodic metal dissolution and the gradual adsorption of inhibitor molecules at the metal/solution interface. However, the OCP curves do not consistently follow a negative trend. Indeed, in certain instances, shifts toward more positive values were observed, particularly for the non-inhibited sample. We can be attributed to factors specific to the sample and the concentration of the inhibitors. It is possible that, for certain inhibitor concentrations, the interaction with the metal surface leads to a more complex variation in the corrosion potential, influencing both anodic and cathodic reactions. In these cases, the effect of the inhibitors may be less pronounced in terms of negative OCP shift or may even cause slight anodic polarization under certain conditions. It is also noteworthy that no linear relationship was observed between inhibitor concentration and the magnitude of the potential shift, suggesting that other factors, such as molecular configuration, steric hindrance, electronic properties, or adsorption kinetics, may influence the adsorption behavior. Additionally, the temporal evolution of the OCP indicates the gradual removal of native iron oxide layers (Fe2O3, Fe3O4). In the presence of inhibitors, the OCP stabilizes after approximately 30 min, suggesting that adsorption equilibrium is reached and surface sites are saturated.

Potentiodynamic polarization tests were performed by varying the working electrode potential at a fixed scan rate. Fig. 8 shows the polarization curves in 1 M HCl at room temperature, with and without the inhibitors at different concentrations.

The addition of inhibitors markedly reduces both anodic and cathodic current densities, accompanied by a decrease in anodic dissolution and a shift in the hydrogen evolution potential. The anodic (βa) and cathodic (βc) Tafel slopes obtained for the blank solution and in the presence of the four glass-based inhibitors show only minor variations. The similarity of βa and βc values for all samples indicates that the addition of the glass inhibitors does not significantly modify the kinetics of either the anodic metal dissolution or the cathodic hydrogen evolution reactions [33].

Table 5 outlines the electrochemical properties, such as corrosion current density (icorr), corrosion potential (Ecorr), cathodic and anodic Tafel slopes (βc, βa) and inhibition efficiency. These parameters ae calculated using the polarization curves at various concentrations of the inhibitors P1, P2, P3 and P4.

An evident reduction in corrosion current density (icorr) together with a concomitant enhancement in inhibition efficiency is observed as the inhibitor concentration increases. The optimum efficiency for inhibitors P1– P4 is reached at 400 ppm. The inhibitory efficiency increases as you boost the inhibitor concentration, peaking at 97.5 % at 400 ppm for P1. However, the addition of the studied inhibitors resulted in minimal changes in the Ecorr values, indicating that these compounds have a slight effect on the metal surface potential. Based on the available scientific literature, it is established that when the difference in corrosion potential between the inhibitor and the control solution is below 85 mV. The stability shown in Ecorr suggests that the inhibitors tend to be mixed-type and adsorbent. The inhibitors convey protection by covering the metal surface without significantly impacting the fundamental process of corrosion [34]. Table 6 below is a comparison table summarizing the inhibition efficiency of vanadate-based glasses against corrosion, typically measured for metals like steel in acidic or alkaline environments.

The main focus of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is to study of corrosion inhibition of mild steel in a 1.0 M HCl medium, comparing conditions with and without the addition of inhibitors. Nyquist graphs, as displayed in Fig. 9 , demonstrate how mild steel behaves in these distinct circumstances and with different P1, P2, P3, and P4 concentrations.

The plots depict a single semicircle, implying that charge transfer is the primary mechanism regulating mild steel's corrosion and protection. The topological structure of all Nyquist plots remained the same, suggesting that the incorporation of inhibitors increased the impedance value without having any significant effect on the other electrochemical properties of the system [39,40].

Fig. 10 represents the phase angle and amplitude that match to the Bode trace that was generated for an electrode in a molar hydrochloric acid solution both before and after various amounts of inhibitors were introduced.

Both the solutions, with and without inhibitor, an individual peak in the Bode graph analysis depicted in Fig. 10. The results confirm the equivalent circuit model utilized for the impedance spectra analyses. Further, the Bode graphs show that all four inhibitors have a single maximum phase angle, suggesting that the electrochemical interactions at the metal-solution interface are primarily influenced by the rate of charge transfer [41]. Table 7 lists all of the electrochemical variables that were obtained by fitting Nyquist diagrams [42–44].

Table 7 shows that in the aggressive HCl medium, the charge transfer resistance (Rp) increases with the concentration of the four inhibitors. This rise in Rp is attributed to the formation of a protective layer at the metal/solution interface, which slows down the corrosion process, while Qdl values decrease until an optimal concentration is reached [45]. These changes suggest the formation of a protective layer on the metal surface due to the adsorption of the inhibitor molecules. The decrease in Qdl can be explained by an increase in the double layer thickness or a reduction in the local dielectric constant, both of which interfere with the metal surface's properties. The reduction in double layer capacitance (Cdl) with increasing concentrations of P1, P2, P3, and P4 further confirms the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the metal surface. The decrease in Cdl from 114.0 μF cm−2 in the uninhibited solution to 60.3 μF cm−2, 65.3 μF cm−2, 48.5 μF cm−2, and 71.8 μF cm−2 at 10−3 M for P1, P2, P3, and P4, respectively, indicates that the protective layer formed by the inhibitors reduces the dielectric constant and increases the double layer thickness, thereby limiting ion transfer and improving corrosion resistance [10]. Additionally, the n values decrease with increasing concentrations of P1, P2, P3, and P4. This can be explained by the reduction in the surface heterogeneity of the mild steel due to the uniform adsorption of the inhibitor molecules. As the concentration of the inhibitors increases, the inhibition efficiency improves. At a concentration of 400 ppm, the inhibition efficiency reaches a maximum of 97.5 % for P1, 97.2 % for P2, 96.9 % for P3, and 90.7 % for P4. These efficiency values indicate the extent to which each inhibitor reduces the corrosion rate.

We can as well draw a relationship between the physical parameters of the glasses and their corrosion inhibition performance. The physical properties of vanadate-based glasses, including density and molar volume intrinsically govern their corrosion inhibition performance by influencing both structural stability and electrochemical interactions at the metal-electrolyte interface. Higher-density glasses exhibit tighter atomic packing, which reduces porosity and limits electrolyte penetration, thereby enhancing barrier protection. Similarly, lower molar volume correlates with reduced free volume in the glass network, obstructing diffusion pathways for aggressive ions like Cl−.

The conclusion that P1 is more effective than P2, P3, and P4 is supported by the high correlation that has been observed between the inhibitory efficiencies derived from several analytical techniques, including the Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and the Potentiodynamic Polarization (PDP). This association demonstrates the consistency and dependability of P1's inhibitory performance across a range of assessment techniques.

Adsorption isotherms, which describe the way molecules or ions interact with a surface, are frequently used to study inhibition mechanisms, particularly in corrosion or adsorption research. When exploring corrosion inhibitors, the most widely employed adsorption isotherms are Langmuir, Temkin, Frumkin and Freundlich [46–49]:

Here, Kads, Cinh and θ represent adsorption-desorption constant, inhibitors concentration and surface coverage, respectively.

The EIS measurements were preferred for testing the isotherm because they offers real-time, non-destructive knowledge on the inhibitor-metal interface. By applying a low-amplitude AC signal that very slightly disturbs the adsorbed inhibitor layer, EIS, unlike other methods, enables precise measurement of surface coverage (θ) through variations in charge-transfer resistance. Consequently, EIS data can be accurately fitted to adsorption isotherm models such as Langmuir and Temkin, allowing quantitative evaluation of inhibitor efficiency and interaction with the metal surface.
Fig. 11 illustrates the fitting of the Langmuir, Temkin, Freundlich, and Frumkin adsorption isotherms for mild steel in 1 M HCl at 298 K in the presence of the inhibitors.
Table 8 summarizes the adsorption parameters of the investigated glass-based inhibitors on mild steel under the same experimental conditions, as obtained from the Langmuir adsorption isotherm.

Among the different adsorption isotherms assessed, the Langmuir model was found to provide the best fit for describing the adsorption behavior of the inhibitors on mild steel surfaces. This model establishes a linear relationship between C/θ and C, with slopes for all investigated compounds remaining below unity. Such deviations suggest that adsorption on the metal surface is not perfectly ideal, likely due to surface heterogeneity or weak lateral interactions among adsorbed species.

The Langmuir isotherm exhibited the highest correlation coefficient (R2 ≈ 1), and the resulting plots for the four inhibitors (P1, P2, P3, and P4) were nearly identical, confirming the model's suitability. According to this model, each inhibitor molecule occupies a single active site on the metal surface. From the data presented in Table 8, obtained from the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the standard free energy of adsorption (
Δ G
a d s
) values for the four inhibitors range between −16.8 kJ/mol and −24.8 kJ/mol. The negative sign of
Δ G
a d s
indicates that the adsorption process occurs spontaneously and primarily through physical adsorption, involving electrostatic interactions between the inhibitor molecules and the steel surface [50]. The magnitude of these
Δ G
a d s
values also implies that some degree of chemical interaction may accompany the physical adsorption process. Furthermore, Table 8 reveals that the adsorption equilibrium constants
K
a d s
are particularly high for inhibitor P3, suggesting a strong affinity of this compound for the steel surface. This implies that the inhibitor molecules remain stably adsorbed, thereby offering effective protection against corrosion [51]. Overall, these results confirm that the inhibitors exhibit strong and stable adsorption characteristics, accounting for their efficiency in mitigating corrosion under aggressive conditions.

The potentiodynamic study also enabled us to explore the impact of temperature on the inhibition efficiency of all the inhibitors prepared. Fig. 12 shows the polarization curves acquired of mild steel in 1 M HCl with and without the inhibitors evaluated at an immersion period over 30 min in the temperature between 298K and 328K [52].

The curves display the Tafel regions, and it is evident that both the anodic and cathodic branches increase with rising temperature. The parallel nature of the cathodic segments indicates that the same activation-controlled process governs the reduction of H+ ions on the steel surface across the examined temperature range.

The corrosion potential
E
c o r r
exhibits minor shifts toward cathodic values at different temperatures. Table 9 summarizes the inhibition efficiencies (E%) and the corresponding electrochemical parameters for steel corrosion in 1 M HCl, both in the absence and presence of inhibitors at various temperatures.

It is apparent from Table 9 that the current densities rise as the temperature rises. When the latter happens, this usually promotes both the desorption of the incorporated inhibitors and the decomposition of the metal [53]. According to the results, the
E
c o r r
slightly decreases as the temperature increases. The decrease in inhibition efficiency with rising temperature can be attributed to the acceleration of electrochemical reactions at the steel/electrolyte interface. At elevated temperatures, the kinetic energy of corrosive species Cl− ions increases, enhancing their diffusion toward the metal surface and promoting the anodic dissolution of iron. Additionally, higher temperatures may cause partial or complete desorption of the adsorbed inhibitor molecules from the mild steel surface, reducing the protective film's coverage and leaving more active sites vulnerable to corrosion. The thermal stability of the inhibitor-metal surface complex can also deteriorate at high temperatures, weakening the adsorption process and diminishing the inhibitor's barrier effect. In essence, the observed reduction in inhibition efficiency at higher temperatures could be from a combination of inhibitor desorption, accelerated corrosion kinetics, and breakdown of the protective film. From a practical perspective, improving inhibitor performance at elevated temperatures may require strategies such as chemical modification to enhance adsorption strength, incorporation of functional groups capable of forming stronger chemisorptive bonds, or the use of synergistic additives that promote surface coverage and thermal stability. Encapsulation techniques or the development of mixed inhibitor systems have also been reported in the literature as effective approaches to mitigate thermal degradation and maintain protective efficiency. To conclude, in our opinion, these substances are better suited to prevent corrosion of mild steel in 1 M HCl at 298K.

Fig. 13 provide a clear picture of the variations of the logarithm of the corrosion current density in relation to the inverse of temperature (1000/T). We observe a linear fluctuation. The activation energy
E a
is estimated using the Arrhenius formula as follows [54]:
(12)

)
icorr: The corrosion current density, A: a pre-exposure factor, R: the perfect gas constant.
T: The absolute temperature (K).

The alternate form of the Arrhenius equation may be used in order to calculate the activation entropy (
Δ S
a
) and the activation enthalpy (
Δ H
a
) such as [55]:
(13)

Where h is Planck's constant, N is Avogadro's number.

Fig. 14 highlights the variation of ln (icorr/T) as a function of the inverse of temperature. The graph is a straight line with slope (-
Δ H
a
/R). Extrapolation of these lines gives the values of (Ln (R/Nh) + Sa/R), hence those of
Δ H
a
and
Δ S
a
. The results attained are shown in Table 10.

Activation energy (
E a
) values increased both before and after the compounds under study were added, as Table 10 illustrates. The physical adsorption of these inhibitors onto the metal surface could be explained by this increase [56]. Actually, the occurrence of a weak chemical interaction between the inhibitor molecules and the mild steel surface is linked to a wider energy barrier to the corrosion process in the inhibited solution [57]. Furthermore, mild steel corrosion is an endothermic process, meaning that extra energy is needed for the oxidation reaction to proceed, as implied by the positive values of
Δ H
a
[58]. This endothermic nature implies that the presence of these inhibitors makes corrosion more challenging. When the entropies (
Δ S
a
) are negative, it suggests a less chaotic state.

This work addressed the elaboration and characterization of the glass (0.25-x) Bi2O3 - x Nb2O5- 0.75V2O5 for 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.15 synthesized through conventional powder melting, as well as its application as a corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in acidic environments. X-ray diffraction confirmed the amorphous nature of the glass, while density measurements revealed a decrease when Nb2O5 replaced Bi2O3, attributed to the lower molar mass and density of niobium compared to bismuth. Spectroscopic analyses (Raman and IR) further demonstrated structural modifications in the glass network, leading to reduced polymerization upon the incorporation of Nb2O5.

In the corrosion inhibition study, the four glass with compositions (0.25-x) Bi2O3 - x Nb2O5- 0.75V2O5 for 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.15 were evaluated in 1 M HCl, demonstrating effective protection for mild steel. Polarization measurements indicated mixed-type inhibition with cathodic predominance, while electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) revealed increased charge transfer resistance (Rct) and decreased double-layer capacitance (C dl), suggesting inhibitor adsorption on the steel surface. Inhibition efficiency reached 97.5 % at 400 ppm for P1 but decreased with rising temperature, consistent with physical adsorption behavior supported by increased activation energy (E a). This table (Table 11
) summarizes the conditions in which the highest inhibition efficiency is achieved for the compound P1 among all tested parameters.

These findings highlight the potential of the studied glasses as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors, offering insights into how compositional changes influence both glass structure and corrosion inhibition mechanisms. However, further research is needed to explore long-term stability and industrial scalability. Future studies could also investigate synergistic effects with other inhibitors or the development of composite coatings to improve durability under harsher conditions.
